title: Macramé coaster set
subtitle: Set of four square coasters at about 10 x 10 cm in 3 mm cotton cord
excerpt: Four square macramé coasters each about 10 x 10 cm, made from 3 mm cotton cord. Cords are mounted with lark's head knots on a small starter ring or cord loop, knotted in alternating square knots across a grid, gathered below with a tight gathering knot, and finished with a short 3 cm fringe.
sourceNotes: Observation-led from the mid-20th-century macramé revival. The alternating-square-knot grid pattern is the canonical beginner macramé body structure. The gathering-knot finish is standard across the tradition. Structural knot references in Graumont and Hensel, Encyclopedia of Knots and Fancy Rope Work (1939), Cornell Maritime Press.

glossaryTerms.definition: A two-loop mounting knot used to attach a folded cord to a ring, dowel, or holding cord. The cord is folded in half, the loop is passed over the mount from front to back, and both tails are pulled through the loop and tightened. The standard starting knot for macramé panels and coasters.

A grid pattern made by tying square knots in groups of four cords, then offsetting the next row so each new knot straddles cords from two adjacent groups. The alternating offset produces a diagonal diamond lattice and also distributes the cord tension evenly across the width of the piece, which makes it well suited for flat coasters.

A wrapping knot that collects multiple hanging cords into a single bound bundle. A short separate working cord wraps tightly around all the tails several times, then its end is threaded through the wrapped coils and pulled to lock the knot. The result is a compact cylindrical knot from which the fringe hangs below.

content.text: These four coasters are each about 10 x 10 cm square, knotted in 3 mm natural cotton macramé cord. Eight cords per coaster are mounted with
lark's head knots
onto a small wooden ring or a looped starter cord, then worked in
alternating square knots
across four rows to build the grid. A tight
gathering knot

below the last row collects all 16 tails into a bound finish, and a 3 cm fringe hangs below. Use a 25 to 35 mm wooden ring as the mount (the ring sits neatly at the centre-top of the finished coaster and can be left visible or hidden by tucking it to the back), or use an 8 cm loop of the same cord as a starter cord that is removed after mounting. Each coaster needs 8 cords at 60 cm each, plus one short gathering cord at 30 cm. Total cord per coaster: 510 cm. Total for the set: 2040 cm, about 20 m.
What you need

items.name: 3 mm cotton macramé cord, natural
items.qty: 22 m (20 m for the set plus a few cord lengths in reserve)
items.substitution: 3 mm single-strand cord produces a denser coaster; twisted 3-ply gives a slightly softer surface

items.name: Small wooden rings, 25 to 35 mm
items.substitution: A looped starter cord of the same diameter works as well; remove the loop after the first knot row has set the shape

content.text: The knots, step by step
Each coaster uses three knot types. The lark's head mounts the eight cords on the ring. The alternating square knot builds the grid body. The gathering knot collects the tails below the final row.

Lark's head: fold a cord in half and drape the fold over the ring from front to back. Pull both tails through the loop and pull snugly against the ring. Repeat for all eight cords, packing them close together on the ring.

definition.caption: Lark's head: fold cord over the ring, pull both tails through the loop and tighten.

content.text: Square knot: using four cords (two outer working cords, two inner filler cords), take the left working cord over both filler cords and under the right working cord. Take the right working cord under both filler cords and up through the loop formed by the left cord. Pull both working cords outward to seat the first half-knot. Reverse the move for the second half: left cord under the fillers, right cord over and through the new loop. Pull evenly to complete the square.

definition.caption: Square knot: two working cords around two filler cords, tied in two opposing half-knot moves.

content.text: Alternating square knot: tie the first row with square knots in two groups of four cords across the full width (cords 1-4 and cords 5-8 from left to right, numbering them after mounting, gives 16 working ends from 8 lark's head cords). The second row ties one square knot using cords 3, 4, 5, and 6, straddling the gap between the first-row groups. Alternate the grouping in each row to build the lattice grid.

definition.caption: Alternating-square knot: offset each row by one cord pair to build the diagonal lattice grid.

content.text: Gathering knot: cut a short 30 cm cord. Hold it against the back of the hanging tails about 1 cm below the last row of knots. Leave a short tail pointing upward; let the rest hang down. Wrap the longer end tightly around all 16 tails and the short tail, moving upward, for six to eight close wraps. Thread the working end through the loop at the top formed by the short upward tail. Pull the short tail downward to lock both ends inside the wraps. Trim both ends flush with the base of the knot.

definition.caption: Gathering knot: a wrapped-and-secured coil that collects all tails before the fringe.

content.text: Method
Step 1: Cut the cords

For one coaster, cut eight working cords at 60 cm each and one gathering cord at 30 cm. Use the cord-measuring tape to mark each length. Cut cleanly with sharp scissors. Repeat to prepare all four sets of cords before starting to knot.
Step 2: Mount the cords on the ring

Pin the wooden ring to the macramé board with a T-pin through the ring. Mount all eight cords with lark's head knots, packing them tightly side by side so no gap is visible between knots. After mounting you will have 16 working ends hanging below the ring. Pull the ring to check that the knots are evenly distributed: the ring should sit level without tilting to one side. If one side has more cords than the other, redistribute before beginning the first row.
Step 3: Tie the alternating square knot grid

Number the working ends 1 through 16 from left to right. Row 1: tie a square knot on cords 1, 2, 3, 4 (cords 1 and 4 are working cords, 2 and 3 are filler cords). Tie a second square knot on cords 5, 6, 7, 8. Tie a third on cords 9, 10, 11, 12. Tie a fourth on cords 13, 14, 15, 16. Leave 4 to 5 mm of cord above each knot so the knots sit with a small gap rather than butted against the ring.

Row 2: drop cords 1 and 2, drop cords 15 and 16. Tie a square knot on cords 3, 4, 5, 6. Tie a second on cords 7, 8, 9, 10. Tie a third on cords 11, 12, 13, 14. Leave the same 4 to 5 mm spacing above each knot. The row 2 knots sit between the row 1 knots, creating the offset lattice.

Row 3: repeat Row 1 exactly, tying four knots at the same cord groupings. Row 4: repeat Row 2, tying three knots at the offset groupings. Pin the work flat to the board after each row to check the grid is lying square. The finished knotted section should measure roughly 8 to 9 cm across at its widest point and 7 to 8 cm from the ring to the last row. The coaster may need a fifth or sixth row if the cord is thin or the knot spacing is loose; measure and knot until the square is complete.
Step 4: Tie the gathering knot and trim the fringe

Collect all 16 hanging cord ends below the last row and hold them in a bundle. Lay the gathering cord against the back of the bundle about 1 cm below the final row of knots. Work the gathering knot as described above. Once the knot is locked, trim all 16 fringe ends to 3 cm below the gathering knot, cutting straight across. If you want a soft fringe, comb each tail lightly from the tip upward. If you want a clean cut-cord look, leave the ends untouched. Make all four coasters in the same way.
Variations

Tying the body in straight rows without offsetting (tying each row with the same cord groupings) produces a horizontal bar pattern instead of a diagonal lattice. The bar pattern is slightly denser and sits flatter, which gives a firmer surface for a coaster that will hold a heavy mug.

A contrasting colour on one or two working cord pairs adds a stripe to the lattice grid. A cream body with a single terracotta working cord pair shows that colour at every knot in the group. The result is a diagonal stripe running across the piece.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The coaster is not square; one side is longer or the grid pulls toward a corner
items.cause: The knot spacing was uneven, or the cords on one side were tensioned more firmly than the other
items.fix: After each row, pin both outer edges of the work to the board at equal measurements from the ring. A board with a grid printed on it helps check that both sides advance at the same rate. If the piece has already pulled out of square, ease the tighter cords outward gently before tying the next row.

items.problem: The gathering knot slides upward and the fringe tails pull free when the coaster is handled
items.cause: The locking step was incomplete: the short upward tail was not pulled down firmly enough to lock both cord ends inside the wraps
items.fix: Pull the short tail firmly and steadily until it stops moving. If the knot has already slipped, undo the wraps, add one or two extra wraps, and re-lock. Keep the wraps very close together; spaced wraps do not grip as firmly.

items.problem: The coaster surface is uneven with some knots sitting raised and others flat
items.cause: Inconsistent tension: some knots were pulled firmly, others loosely
items.fix: After each square knot, pull both working cords outward with the same force. Practise on a spare set of cords first to find a consistent pull pressure, then apply it throughout the piece.
